coredata.dc:description: Today’s carbohydrate-based bio-carriers have attracted more attention as drug delivery agents, so they have brilliant biocompatibility, as well as, controlled drug release potential. Considering these, the porous starch (PS) with the honeycomb network was in situ synthesized in the presence of pre-synthesized carbon dots (CDs/PS). The SEM displayed the porous structure of CDs/PS with a uniform dispersion of CDs in the polymer matrix. BET analysis obtained the diameter of the porous sites at about 37.319 nm. After oxaliplatin (OXP) loading the OXP/CDs/PS was coated with the pectin (Pec) in a green strategy ((OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec). The drug release studies displayed that the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec has a controlled release performance than OXP/CDs/PS in the simulated pH of oral drug delivery condition. The biocompatibility of the (CDs/PS)@Pec and its cancer cell-killing capability after drug loading were approved via MTT test. These results promoted us that the modification of OXP/CDs/PS with Pec achieves a controlled drug release profile and the fabricated system could be suggested as a new delivery system with targeted delivery potential.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent oxaliplatin loaded porous starch coated with pectin biopolymer: Design, fabrication, and controlled drug delivery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Today’s carbohydrate-based bio-carriers have attracted more attention as drug delivery agents, so they have brilliant biocompatibility, as well as, controlled drug release potential. Considering these, the porous starch (PS) with the honeycomb network was in situ synthesized in the presence of pre-synthesized carbon dots (CDs/PS). The SEM displayed the porous structure of CDs/PS with a uniform dispersion of CDs in the polymer matrix. BET analysis obtained the diameter of the porous sites at about 37.319 nm. After oxaliplatin (OXP) loading the OXP/CDs/PS was coated with the pectin (Pec) in a green strategy ((OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec). The drug release studies displayed that the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec has a controlled release performance than OXP/CDs/PS in the simulated pH of oral drug delivery condition. The biocompatibility of the (CDs/PS)@Pec and its cancer cell-killing capability after drug loading were approved via MTT test. These results promoted us that the modification of OXP/CDs/PS with Pec achieves a controlled drug release profile and the fabricated system could be suggested as a new delivery system with targeted delivery potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Colorectal cancer is one of the most common types of cancer in both developed and developing countries (Buczek et al., 2021; Zolfagharian et al., 2022; Lee et al., 2023; Ramamoorthy et al., 2023; Badrloo et al., 2024). In spite of the surgical capability for colorectal cancer treatment, chemotherapy is still the popular choice (Mirdamadian et al., 2022; Saroha et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023; Ghahari et al., 2021; Harris, 2022). OXP is one of the most commonly used anticancer drugs in colorectal cancer therapy. However, its direct administration suffers from some disadvantages and therefore it is imperative to design new drug carriers. To date, various materials have been reported for successful encapsulation of anticancer drugs like OXP, but most of these systems have not been studied for toxicity (Pooresmaeil and Namazi, 2022a; 2022b; 2021; Pooresmaeil et al., 2022; Singh et al., 2021). To tackle the aforementioned issue, natural porous matrices made of polysaccharides may be a good option to load drug molecules (Choi et al., 2022; Javanbakht et al., 2020; Hashemi and Namazi, 2021). Starch is the second distributed most abundant biomass carbohydrate (Jia et al., 2021; Pooresmaeil and Namazi, 2023; Safarpour et al., 2024). Many types of pristine starch granules do have not any pores in their structure to present the spaces for bioactive molecular encapsulation (Lin et al., 2022; Tanan et al., 2021; Esvandi et al., 2020; Namazi and Abdollahzadeh, 2017). Accordingly, this issue encourages researchers to generate porous starch (PS) with several pore sites in its structure (Dragan et al., 2023; Kang et al., 2022). PS is one more applicable form of modified starch with a “honeycomb” structure and a larger specific pore volume that can be obtained through chemical, physical-mechanical, and enzymatic actions. This kind of multifunctional biomaterial received more attention and extensive application owing to its good biocompatibility, micropores sites in the structure, large specific surface area, easy availability, low cost, and adsorption features (Chen et al., 2020; Dura et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2012; Hao et al., 2021). Additionally, the efficiency of PS could improve as a result of its combination with nanomaterials. CDs are novel photoluminescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials composed of sp2 carbon domain having polar functional groups in their chemical structure. Because of their good photo-stability, tunable photoluminescence (PL), solubility in water, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility they have received growing interest in the biomedicine area (Zhang et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2022; Pooresmaeil et al., 2020; Dhongde et al., 2024). Therefore, it is expected that the systems containing both PS and CDs can be a good platform for OXP loading. On the other hand, the oral administration of chemotherapeutic drugs is the main convenient way of their administration that can enhance the patient's convenience. However, the acidic condition of the stomach, and the proteolytic activity of the gastrointestinal tract because of the presence of digestive enzymes are some main challenges for the oral delivery of drugs (Mirdamadian et al., 2022). Several materials have been investigated and utilized as a starting material for specific colon drug delivery. In between, carbohydrate polymers have gained more popularity. For example, Pec biopolymer can successfully act as a pH-sensitive coat because of its special structure and obtain specific colon therapy (Asl et al., 2023; Hassanpouraghdam et al., 2022). Additionally, it was confirmed that the Pec could only be biodegraded by the produced pectinase of intestinal bacteria in the colon, so it has good gastrointestinal stability. This evidence shows that the Pec based gels are the ideal drug carriers for colon-targeted delivery (Rafe et al., 2022; Cai et al., 2022). Regeneration, biodegradability, and biocompatibility are some advantages of Pec biopolymer (Shen et al., 2022; Michie et al., 2022). This biopolymer can cross-link through the physical or chemical bonds to form a three-dimensional network structure. So, the prepared beads via ionotropic gelation with divalent cations such as Ca2+, Zn2+, and Ba2+ are more favorable owing to their non-toxicity (Cui et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2019).

Considering the above-mentioned facts, the main purpose of this project was to obtain a colon-speciﬁc delivery of OXP relying on the polysaccharide properties. Therefore, inspired by the above aim and by considering the synergistic effect of CDs/PS combined and a Pec coat, for the first time the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec was prepared as a new effective drug carrier for colon cancer therapy. Up to now, several studies have been done to fabricate carbohydrate-based drug delivery systems, but to the best of our knowledge, there are not any reports on selecting CDs/PS as the OXP loading platform and then coating the hybrid with Pec to develop biodegradable colon-specific delivery system. The structural investigation was performed via FT-IR, XRD, SEM, and EDX mapping in detail and reported. Then the efficiency of (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec for in vitro colon-controlled therapy of the OXP was studied. The in vitro OXP delivery investigations approved the better potential of CDs/PS for OXP loading and the positive effect of Pec coating in reaching the colon-speciﬁc delivery. Consequently, the attained results showed that the prepared system in the current research is a new carbohydrate-based drug delivery platform with novelty from the application’s and component's point of view, and could be proposed as a controlled drug delivery system.

Garlic was procured from the local fruit shop, Tabriz, Iran. Starch, absolute ethanol, zinc acetate, sodium chloride (NaCl), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and paraformaldehyde were purchased from Merck. Pec, 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazoliumbromide (MTT) were the products of Sigma-Aldrich. Roswell park memorial institute (RPMI) 1640 growth medium, fetal bovine serum albumin (FBS), trypsin, and penicillin-streptomycin were attained from Gibco. OXP (vial 50 mg/5 mL) was purchased from Estragen Pharma, Iran. HT 29 human colon cancer cell lines were provided by the Iranian National Cell Bank (Pasteur Institute, Tehran, Iran). All reagents were of analytical grade and utilized as received.

For the synthesis of CDs, initially, about 1.5 g of the crushed garlic cloves were added to distilled water (45 mL). The mixture was stirred magnetically for 30 min to well mixed. The suspension was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave chamber, and after seeding, it was heated at 200 °C for 180 min. The appearance of the brown color solution indicated the formation of CDs which then were naturally cold to room temperature. The product was centrifuged and filtered to dislodge the deposit. After that, the solution was subjected to dialysis against ultra-pure water, using a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da dialysis bag to obtain the pure CDs aqueous solution for further use (Zhao et al., 2015).

The preparation method of the CDs/PS hybrid was adjusted according to (Li et al., 2021; Oliyaei et al., 2020) with slight modifications. Typically, 1 g of starch was added to 20 mL of the CDs aqueous solution while stirring. The temperature of the reaction was fixed at 90 °C and the agitation was continued at this condition for 90 min. After complete gelatinization, the resultant mixture was refrigerated for 48 h at 5 °C to be gel. The gel was cut into cubes with 1 cm3 dimension and soaked in absolute ethanol at room temperature. After 1 h, the mixture was decantated. The operation was repeated thrice to reach a porous structure. Finally, the product was freeze-dried to obtain a dry CDs/PS hybrid. Neat PS was also synthesized in the same mentioned way without the addition of any CDs.

OXP loaded CDs/PS hybrid was prepared via the dispersion of CDs/PS powder in OXP solution (1000 mg/mL). Thereafter, the mixture was sonicated for 5 min and left under shaking at room temperature in dark conditions. After 72 h, the OXP/CDs/PS particles were isolated carefully from the mixture using a centrifuge at 6000 rpm and room temperature and rinsed to remove the adsorbed drug. The collected filtrate of OXP loading and rinsing solutions was used to measure the residual concentrations of OXP and loading percentages were estimated employing Eq. 1 and 2. Eventually, the separated OXP/CDs/PS particles were kept for further usage after drying under a vacuum (Abukhadra et al., 2020; Vivek et al., 2014). The OXP loading was performed three times and the average of the obtained values were reported.
(1)
OXP loading capacity
% w

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gastro-resistant encapsulation of OXP/CDs/PS into Pec beads
ce:section.ce:para: OXP/CDs/PS was encapsulated in a Pec bead matrix via the ionotropic gelation method. Initially, the OXP/CDs/PS (0.1 g) was homogeneously dispersed in the aqueous solution (2 mL) with the help of an ultrasonic bath. Pec powder (0.5 g) was added to the water solution (6.33 mL) and then agitated for 2 h at room temperature. Then the previously prepared OXP/CDs/PS suspension was added to the polymer solution to obtain the Pec solution at the concentration of 6 % (W/V). In the next step, the mixture was further sonicated on a bath sonicator and after 1 h magnetic stirring was left undisturbed. The bubble-free mixture was dropped into a cross-linking solution of zinc acetate (50 mL, 12 % W/V), using a syringe while stirring slowly. The instantaneously formed beads were left in the cross-linking solution for 30 min under slight magnetic stirring to cure. The excess zinc ions were removed through ﬁltration and three times washings with distilled water. Lastly, the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec beads were dried at room temperature for subsequent performance testing.

The swelling behavior of the prepared (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec beads was studied via the gravimetric way in the simulated oral condition at 37 °C, a simulated gastric fluid (SGF, pH 1.2, 2 h), the simulated first zone of intestinal fluid (SIF, pH 6.8, 2 h), and also simulated colonic fluid (SCF, pH 7.8, 4 h). Then the corresponding swelling (%) at different time intervals (t) was calculated by using the following Eq. (3). The reported swelling percentages are the average of three experiments.
(3)
Swelling
%
=
W s − W d
W d
×
100
Here Wd is the weight of the dry sample at the initial time and Ws is the weight of the swelled sample at time t.

The in vitro release of the OXP drug from the OXP/CDs/PS and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec samples was carried out at 37 °C in simulated oral drug delivery conditions from the pH viewpoint. In this regard, the dry drug loaded samples were immersed in simulated gastric fluid (pH 1.2), simulated small intestinal fluid (pH 6.8), and simulated colon fluid (pH 7.4) respectively for 2 h, 2 h, and 4 h. In detail, the pre-weighed drug loaded samples were immersed in a buffer solution with pH 1.2 and kept under low orbital stirring in an orbital shaker incubator. At several time intervals, 2.5 mL of the solution containing the released drug was withdrawn and the same volume of fresh solution was added to keep the solution volume constant. After this 2 h, the whole buffer was replaced with a buffer with pH 6.8, and sampling was performed similarly to pH 1.2. After this 2 h, the whole buffer was replaced with a buffer with pH 7.4, and sampling was performed similarly to pH 1.2 and 6.8. The absorbance of the drug in each of the withdrawn solutions was examined by a UV-Vis spectrophotometer and converted to the drug concentration using a standard calibration curve constructed from a series of OXP solutions with well-known concentrations. In the following, the achieved concentration was converted to the mass of the released drug by considering the release media volume. Finally, the drug release % was obtained as Eq. 4. The drug release was studied three times and the listed percentages are the average of these three repetitions.
(4)
Drug release
% w
=
Amount of released OXP
Amount of loaded OXP
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the point of zero charge (PZC) pH (pHPZC)
ce:section.ce:para: The pH for the point of zero charge as the parameter that can describe the condition when the electrical charge density on the bead surface is zero was calculated from the difference between the initial and final pHs of the immersion solution. Typically, dried (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec were added to flasks containing NaCl solution (30 mL, 0.1 mol L−1) with specific pHs (from 2 to 9), pHs were adjusted to a certain value with dilute HCl or NaOH solution (0.1 mol L−1). The flasks containing the mixture were shaken for 24 h at room temperature to equilibrium reach. Then the samples were withdrawn from each vial and the final pH (pHf) of the solutions was determined immediately. The difference between the initial (pHi) and final pHs (ΔpH) was plotted against pHi and pHPZC was described as the pH where the point where the pHi is equal to the pHf.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro radical scavenging activity test
ce:section.ce:para:
The antioxidant activity of CDs, CDs/PS, and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec was evaluated using the common DPPH assay. Briefly, at first, the fresh stock solution of DPPH was prepared. In the next step, the dispersion of the samples (2 mg, 2 mL) in methanol was mixed with 2 mL of a stock DPPH. The mixture was shaken for 30 min on a shaker incubator in the dark medium at 37 °C. After 30 min, the mixture was centrifuged and the absorbance of the supernatant was determined at 517 nm. The reduction in the absorbance after treatment with the samples can approve their radical scavenging activity. Finally, the antioxidant activity of the samples was defined as Eq. 5.
(5)
DPPH scavenging effect = (
A
0
−
A
1
A
0
) × 100
In Eq. 5 the A0 and A1 respectively are the symbols for the absorbance of the standard solution of DPPH and sample solutions in the same medium.

The hemocompatibility of the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec was analyzed by employing the spectroscopy technique based on published references with some modifications (Zhou et al., 2022). Typically, the freshly prepared blood was centrifuged for 10 min at 2000 rpm to obtain a high concentration of erythrocytes by removing the supernatant serum. Sterile normal saline solution (5 mL) was added to the condensed red blood cells, and the mixture was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 5 min. After thrice repeating the aforementioned washing procedure, the erythrocytes were diluted to the final density of 10 % (v/v). Then, erythrocyte stock (500 μL) and the sample extract dispersion (31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 μg mL−1) were poured into a 2 mL microtube, mildly mixed, and incubated at 37 °C for 60 min with vibration (at 100 rpm). After 1 h, the mixture was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for another 10 min and the absorbance of the supernatants was recorded at 540 nm via spectrophotometer. Normal saline solution and deionized water respectively were considered the negative and positive control. Eq. 6 obtained the hemolysis percentage.
(6)
Hemolysis
%
=
Absorbance of sample − Absorbance of negative control
Absorbance of posetive control − Absorbance of negative control
× 100

The cytotoxicity of the (CDs/PS)@Pec, free OXP, and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec samples were explored toward HT 29 cells as a model of human colon cancer cell lines through the MTT method. Typically, the selected HT 29 cells with a density of 1 × 104 cells were cultured in the 96-well plate containing a complete medium comprising RPMI 1640, FBS (10 % v/v), and Penstrep (1 % v/v). The seeded cells were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO2 atmosphere. In the next step, the old medium was changed with various concentrations of the samples in the complete medium and the cells were incubated in the previous conditions. After 8 h the unconsumed samples were removed and 200 µL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well. The incubation was followed for 4 h. After this time, the MTT was removed from the wells and 200 µL of DMSO was added to dissolve the formed crystals. The plates were measured by an ELISA plate reader at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm and then the cell viability was measured. The MTT assay was accomplished in three replicates and the reported data are the average of them.

In this study, a new oral drug delivery system was prepared for the in vitro colon-specific chemotherapy. Because encapsulation is one of the effective techniques to protect the bioactive compounds, ensure stability, and obtain controlled delivery, the OXP was capsulated in the CDs/PS. 30.1 % was determined for the values of the OXP encapsulation efﬁciency. Then to provide a suitable colon-specific drug delivery system, the OXP/CDs/PS was incorporated with Pec biopolymer employing a green strategy. The schematic of the performed experimental work is provided in Fig. 1. The results of the characterization studies confirmed the success of the overall process. Finally, the observed drug release pattern, hemolysis assay, and in vitro cellular study suggested the potential of (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec hydrogel bead for use in colon-specific drug delivery.

To confirm the successful hybridization of the CDs with PS, loading of OXP on CDs/PS, as well as the coating of OXP/CDs/PS into the Pec hydrogel, the Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were recorded employing the Bruker Instruments (FT-IR, model Aquinox 55, Germany) in the region of 400–4000 cm−1. The obtained spectra are reported in Fig. 2a. CDs displayed an intense and wide peak at 3426 cm−1 which is related to the stretching vibration of -OH functional groups. Moreover, this spectrum displayed the peaks at 2925 cm−1, 1712 cm−1, 1625 cm−1, 1408 cm−1, and 681 cm−1 which respectively ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-H, CO, CC, C-N, and C-S group. The spectrum of the neat PS displayed the stretching vibration of the -OH, CH2 related to anhydroglucose ring, -C-O of the -C-O-H bonds, and -C-O of the C-O-C anhydroglucose ring at 3438 cm−1, 2927 cm−1, 1161 cm−1, 1085 cm−1, 991 cm−1, 927 cm−1, and 852 cm−1 respectively (Oliyaei et al., 2020). Interestingly, in the case of CDs/PS, the obtained FT-IR spectrum is similar to the combining of CDs and PS FT-IR bands with more similarity to PS, which could be a good sign for the CDs dispersion in the PS network. Also, in this spectrum, a slight shift was observed with respect to each precursor that is related to the possible interactions between them. In the FT-IR spectrum of OXP, the detected characteristic peaks at 3411 cm−1, 2926 cm−1, 2862 cm−1, and 1381 cm−1 respectively are respected to the stretching vibration of -NH, -CH, and C-O. This molecule also presented the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of -COO- at 1701 cm−1 and 1673 cm−1. The associated peaks related to Pt-N vibrations appeared at 524 cm−1 and 620 cm−1 of the OXP FT-IR spectrum. Also, the characteristic peaks of bending vibration of COO- and Pt-O vibrations or their combination forms were detected at 892 cm−1, 781 cm−1, and 564 cm−1. In comparison with neat CDs/PS the OXP/CDs/PS presented new peaks attributed to OXP vibration that can be a good reason for OXP loading on it. The FT-IR spectrum of pure Pec showed the bands at 3415 cm−1, 2951 cm−1, 1746 cm−1, 1559 cm−1, and 1448 cm−1 which respectively are responsible for the stretching vibration of the -OH, -CH, CO (in -COOCH3), -COO- asymmetric stretching, and -COO- symmetric stretching. The FT-IR spectrum of the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec contained the representative peaks of both OXP/CDs/PS and Pec, thus indicating that OXP/CDs/PS was successfully embedded inside the Pec matrix. All these results approved the successful synthesis of (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec in a green way.

The crystallinity of the samples is usually evaluated by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Therefore the Tongda TD-3700 X-ray diffractometer was used to obtain the XRD spectra of the samples. According to the XRD pattern (Fig. 2b), there is one broad peak at 2θ ∼23.5° of synthesized CDs, which is in line with the previously reported spectrum for CDs. The detected peak corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic-like lattice space of highly disordered CDs, due to the presence of a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, like -OH and -COOH which lead to a paucity in substantial order. The interlayer d spacing for CDs was calculated to be ca. 3.76 nm (Sravya et al., 2022). For the CDs encapsulated within the PS, a wider peak appeared at 2θ ∼14–32° that arose from the formation of the amorphous structure due to possible cross-linking of the starch chains with CDs in the used synthesis condition. The absence of sharp peaks of OXP in the OXP/CDs/PS indicates that the drug is well dispersed in the OXP/CDs/PS (Tummala et al., 2016). The Pec coated OXP/CDs/PS loaded hydrogel beads presented a wide peak at a 2θ position in the range of 24°-33° indicating the complete blending and cross-linking of the raw materials. Therefore, in this study, it can be suggested that CDs could be successfully entrapped into the PS matrix and well dispersed in Pec as bead form after drug loading on it.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of the surface morphology and elemental composition of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: The different factors like constituent, porosity, size, and morphology play a significant role in drug delivery efficiency. Therefore, the surface morphology of the samples was investigated by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, MIRA3 FEG-SEM), and the gained images are reported in Fig. 3. The obtained SEM image of the CDs displayed their spherical shape as small dots which are almost uniform. The average diameter of synthesized CDs was calculated at ranges from about 9–12 nm. The surface morphology of PS and CDs/PS are shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c. As it is visible, both samples have a porous structure that is below 100 nm. So, the micrograph for PS is similar to that for CDs/PS without CDs dispersion on it. In fact, the CDs/PS indicates that the CDs homogeneously are well dispersed on the polymer matrix. The change in the surface structure of CDs/PS with loading the OXP was observed, confirming that they only partially adsorbed on the surface, but also loaded into the cavities (Günter and Popeyko, 2016; Zhao et al., 2021). The OXP/CDs/PS coated with Pec displayed a sphere-like morphology with a slightly wrinkled and rugged surface as well as pores or cracks on it, which may be attributed to the dispersion of CDs/PS on the Pec microsphere. The particle size of the system is approximately 20 μm (Fig. 3e).

For the semi-quantitatively elemental analysis, the synthesized samples were identified and confirmed using energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, Fig. 4. This analysis demonstrated that the PS is mainly composed of O and C elements. The detected elements for the CDs/PS were C, O, N, and S) and show the successful dispersion of CDs onto PS. After OXP loading the Pt peak was also detected in the spectrum of OXP/CDs/PS that approved its successful loading on CDs/PS. As can be seen, the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec exhibited a strong corresponding peak of Zn in addition to the prominent peaks of the C, O, N, Pt, and S that indicate the crosslinking with Zn ions.

Additionally, the EDX elemental mapping is performed for (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec to show the successful dispersion of CDs/PS within the Pec network. It is worth noting that the related elements are uniformly distributed in the composite as evidenced by EDX mapping ( Fig. 5). In the elemental distribution mapping, C, N, O, S, Zn, and Pt atoms also evenly distributed the whole structures, which indicated that CDs/PS and OXP are compatible well with the hydrogel beads.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of point of zero charge of (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec bead
ce:section.ce:para: The pHpzc is one of the main factors that can affect the drug release behavior of each pH-sensitive system. Fig. 6a shows the determination of pHpzc value for the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec bead. The performed experiments obtained the pHpzc to be 3.9 which shows the nanocomposite hydrogel is neutral at pH 3.9. At the pH value above zero-point charge (pH < pHpzc), (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec bead surface is negatively charged. While in a medium with a pH lower than pHpzc, the surface is positively charged. Similar results have also been reported in the previously published works. To conclude, the obtained pHpzc value can reveal the main effect of Pec and approve the suitability of (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec bead for use in oral drug delivery applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BET surface area and pore size analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To further approve the existence of the pore sites in CDs/PS structure, determine their size, and obtain the SBET, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm study was performed employing the TriStar II 3020, Micromeritics Instrument. BET-plot, N2 adsorption/desorption, and BJH-plot respectively are presented in Fig. 6b, Fig. 6c, and Fig. 6d. As obtainable in Fig. 6, the structural characteristics demonstrated the apparent type IV isotherm hysteresis loop for CDs/PS. As observeable the pore sizes in the sample are mainly distributed between 1 and 100 nm with a mean diameter of about 37.319 nm. This pore structure is beneficial to the improvement of drug loading performance. Interestingly, the BET specific surface area and total pore volumes were about 80.533 m2/g and 0.7514 cm3/g respectively. Taken together, according to the BET surface area and pore size analysis, the CDs/PS can provide more active sites due to the porous nature of starch and adsorb reactive species due to narrow pore size distribution and large BET surface area.

For the proof of the photoluminescent concept, the aqueous dispersion of the very fine powder of the synthesized CDs, CDs/PS, and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec was evaluated via photoluminescent (PL) spectroscopy employing a Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter. Fig. 7a presented their PL emission spectra at room temperature. The highest PL intensity of CDs was observed at 443 nm when excited at 365 nm. While this value was shifted for CDs/PS and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec bead. Additionally, the PL intensity was reduced for CDs/PS and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec in comparison with CDs. The self-absorbing, partial quenching, inner photoluminescence reabsorption, filter effect, external or internal transformations phenomena, intersystem transitions and extinction effect, and less percentage of fluorescent portion could be proposed as the probable reasons for the observed trend (Javanbakht and Shaabani, 2019). However, based on the observed sufficient PL properties (inset of Fig. 7a), the final system can be proposed as a possible bio-detection device for further future investigations. Digital photographs of (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec aqueous dispersion under 365 nm UV and visible light are depicted in inset Fig. 7a.

The DSC technique commonly is used to examine how the loading of OXP within CDs/PS as a result of the possible interaction between OXP and CDs/PS from the drug crystallinity viewpoint. Fig. 7b displays the obtained differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) thermograms of the neat OXP, as well as the prepared OXP/CDs/PS by Linseis-L63/45 equipment at the heating rate of 10 °C/min. For neat OXP a sharp peak was detected at 299 °C, corresponding to its melting point, showing its crystalline nature. However, when OXP was loaded into the CDs, and CDs/PS was prepared, the peak related to the melting of the OXP crystal was not observed. The findings further show the amorphous dispersion of the entrapped OXP in the OXP/CDs/PS. This result can display the successful entrapment of the OXP in CDs/PS and is in agreement with the outcome of the XRD study (Mirdamadian et al., 2022; Ullah et al., 2019a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro radical scavenging activity of the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec bead
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the inherent antioxidant activity of the garlic, it is expected that the prepared CDs from it display radical scavenging activity. Taking this into account, the antioxidant activity of the CDs, CDs/PS, and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec bead was determined via doing the DPPH test, and the obtained results were presented in Fig. 7c,d. In this assay, the inhibition percentage can be measured via a comparison of the absorbance at 517 nm against the blank. As it is reported, the color of the treated DPPH solution with all three samples was changed from deep violet to light. Considering Eq. 5, the antioxidant percentage of CDs, CDs/PS, and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec bead was determined respectively about 60.7 %, 32.1 %, and 11.7 %. These results verified the good radical scavenging ability of the system which could be applicable for anticancer drug delivery.

Swelling is a diffusion procedure that was driven via the swelling media molecule's affinity to the polymer matrix. This character plays a critical role and determines the system's behavior as a drug carrier. Considering these, the swelling pattern of (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec hydrogel beads was obtained via the gravimetric method with a time of exposure in SGF (pH 1.2, 2 h), SIF (pH 6.8, 2 h), and SCF (pH 7.4, 4 h). The obtained plot is shown in Fig. 8a. It was detected that the beads showed a low percentage of swelling under acidic conditions. However, an increase in pH leads to a higher percentage of swelling, 261.66 %, and 555.67 % respectively at pH 6.8 and pH 7.4. Indeed, when beads were immersed in an aqueous medium with lower pH, the protonation of the carboxylic acid group helped to maintain the polymeric network rigidly, indicating better stability in acid media. However, because the carboxylic functional group of Pec is readily ionizable at elevated pH conditions, deprotonation of the carboxyl group in the matrix caused the loss of the polymer network and induced repulsive forces. This phenomenon allowed more buffer molecules to diffuse into a network which leads to the complete expansion of the material. Based on the finding and because the fast disintegration of drug delivery vehicles in stomach conditions is the main reason for the burst release of drugs, the prepared system could be proposed as an efficient candidate for colon-specific drug delivery owing to diminish drug releases at pH 1.2 by avoiding the system disintegration (Günter and Popeyko, 2016; Nalini et al., 2022; Günter et al., 2018). Since the pH-sensitive swelling pattern is a benefit for drug carriers, the prepared system could be proposed as a hopeful volunteer for use as a specific colon delivery drug carrier.

OXP commonly is used as the first line of therapy against colorectal cancer thereby showing the maximum cytotoxicity against colon cancer cell lines. Additionally, OXP is advantageous over cisplatin due to its better safety profile and activity against cisplatin-resistant cancer cells. Therefore, in this work, the OXP was selected and loaded on CDs/PS. The obtained drug loading capacity and encapsulation efficiency of the CDs/PS were about 36 % and 3.6 % which can be explained by the contribution of the hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interaction which directs its entrapment. Moreover, drug loading may occur in the pore sites of the system that offer more available sites for combining with OXP molecules. Van der Waals forces may also be part of the loading process (Ghalandari et al., 2015; Ding et al., 2021).

Delivery of bioactive substances to a specific site and also maintaining a stable concentration of it before reaching the target site are two essential required properties for each newly presented drug delivery system. Thereby, the in vitro release profile of OXP from OXP/CDs/PS and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec beads was monitored under simulated human gastrointestinal conditions as a function of time, from the pH viewpoint (Fig. 8b). OXP/CDs/PS presented a significant release in an acidic medium. However, it can be observed within the first 2 h, only about 5.10 % of the drug was released in SGF (pH 1.2) for (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec beads. The higher stability owing to the presence of the Pec coat in the structure can be mentioned as the reason for the observed trend. Upon the change of release medium to pH 6.8, the percentage of released drug reached 17.60 %, for the next 2 h. In the following, the replacement of the medium at pH 7.4 reached the OXP release of 36.83 %. The observed trend is consistent with changes in the swelling. From the in vitro release experiments it was noticeable that Pec can reduce the sensitivity to acidic digestion conditions. Therefore the fabricated system could be an efficient carrier and constantly provide the required drug concentration within a safe mode for a longer time.

The blood compatibility of the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec was assessed with a reliable approach. The obtained hemolysis percentage as a result of incubating red blood cells (RBCs) with the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec is shown in Fig. 9a. As reported, the quantitative results displayed the dose-dependent hemolytic action, but, the obtained hemolysis ratio for all of the studied concentrations was lower than 5 %. Because in all of the obtained results, the hemolytic degree is lower than the permissible limit of reported 5 % for biomaterials in contact with blood as per ASTM standard (ASTM F756–2008), it is considered that the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec to be non-hemolytic and therefore desirable for biological utilization. Fig. 9b shows the discrepancies in color by comparing the sample group with the control group. Supernatants of the samples offered faint yellow that related to the resemble of the normal saline solution group. However, RBCs incubated with water obtained bright red owing to leaking without complete cellular structures. Overall results point out that the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec is non-hemolytic and hence it is suitable for biomedicinal applications (Nalini et al., 2022). The reason for the achieved result can be related to the non-toxic nature of the used carbohydrates in the fabricated system structure.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of the free OXP, (CDs/PS)@Pec, and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec beads was investigated at different concentrations relying on the metabolic activity of the HT 29 cells determined by the MTT assay (Fig. 9c). This test measures the mitochondrial activity directly related to cell viability. For all of the samples excitingly, prominent concentration-dependent cytotoxicity on the colon cancer cells was observed and the viability of the cancer cells decreased as a result of the concentration increase. The exposed cells with (CDs/PS)@Pec beads after an incubation time of 8 h had no important cytotoxic effects and cell viability was obtained in the range of 89.4 % in comparison to the control cells that arose from the presence of the biocompatible portions in the structure of the carrier. Both free OXP and (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec displayed cytotoxicity. Free OXP inhibited 18.0 % of cells at a concentration of 50 mg/L while (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec inhibited 58.7 % of cells at the same concentration. Note that the drug-loaded samples showed relatively lower cytotoxicity at the same concentration which might be ascribed to the slight release of OXP. In fact compared with the free OXP, drug-loaded hydrogel beads exhibited controlled inhibition of the studied cell line. These findings are in accordance with the earlier study conducted by Ghalandari et al. (2015); Ullah et al. (2019b).

In summary, for the proof of concept in the development of new anticancer-controlled delivery systems, the porous CDs/PS was prepared and then the OXP as a model of the anticancer drug was successfully loaded on it. Then the OXP/CDs/PS was capped with Pec. In dead, this ternary system was designed to combine advantages from all three used components: (I) CDs which offer the PL and antioxidant features to the final system; (II) the PS matrix, in which the drug is incorporated within it; and (III) prepared Pec coating by ionic gel method protects the OXP at low pH of the stomach. SEM images showed the formation of porous structure for CDs/PS. FT-IR and XRD studies approved that the OXP was encapsulated in the platform. The OXP loading capacity and loading efficiency values were obtained at 3.6 % and 36 %. The observed in vitro OXP release pattern approved that the (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec successfully passed through the simulated stomach and small intestine. The in vitro MTT, DPPH, and hemolysis assay respectively verified the cell compatibility, antioxidant activity, and blood compatibility of the prepared drug carrier. On this basis, this study highlights the potential of the prepared (OXP/CDs/PS)@Pec as a colon-specific chemotherapeutic drug delivery system with good tumor targetability, antioxidant activity, and biocompatibility for colorectal cancer treatment.
Hassan Namazi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Malihe Pooresmaeil: Writing – original draft, Investigation.
